EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sudden increase in winds with gusts to near 40 mph led to a dust storm on Interstate 90 at milepost 196.   Low visibilities in the dust storm contributed to a multi vehicle accident involving five vehicles that were all totaled, along with seven indirect injuries.  Several road and highway closure resulted from the dust storm.

A fetch of deep moisture aimed at the East Slopes of the North Washington Cascades led to heavy snow in the mountains.  Holden Village received 18 inches of snow, with several other higher elevation sites receiving 8 to 12 inches.
Several inches of new snow fell on the Washington side of the Columbia River gorge for areas east of Stevenson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local TV media reported that 6 inches of new snow in Cascade Locks, Oregon, and that 8 inches of snow had fallen in Hood River, Oregon.  It is likely these same conditions affected Stevenson and Carson, Wa, which are across the Columbia River from Cascade Locks, Oregon, as well as areas in Washington that are directly across from Hood River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system brought very heavy rainfall to the region in early January. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff caused flooding along multiple rivers in southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along the Cowlitz and Coweman Rivers which resulted in the evacuation of 4000 people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system brought very heavy rainfall to the region in early January. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff caused flooding along multiple rivers in southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt runoff caused the Grays River near Rosburg to overflow its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front brought heavy snow to central and northeast Washington.  Behind the warm front, shallow cold air remained trapped in the Spokane, Deer Park, and Springdale areas with a mix of freezing rain and freezing drizzle with minor ice accumulations.  In the Moses Lake area several motor vehicle accidents occurred from a combination of snow and fog.  Snow accumulations over central and northeast Washington ranged from three to six inches, except in Leavenworth where as much as ten inches of snow was reported.

Behind the warm front, shallow cold air remained trapped in the Spokane, Deer Park, and Springdale areas with a mix of freezing rain and freezing drizzle with minor ice accumulations.  In the Moses Lake area several motor vehicle accidents occurred from a combination of snow and fog.  Snow accumulations over central and northeast Washington ranged from three to six inches, except in Leavenworth where as much as ten inches of snow was reported.

With abnormally cold air already in place over much of southwest Washington, a storm system advanced across the region, bringing heavy precipitation, which fell as ice over many areas. Many major highways and interstates were closed due to icy conditions causing travel to be treacherous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Transportation closed Interstate 84 from Troutdale to Hood River for dangerous icy conditions on the roadway.  Given the conditions of the storm, it is safe to assume the same dangerous conditions were present on state highway 14 in Washington.  Broadcast media did report the highway was closed to truck traffic between Washougal and White Salmon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 8-20 inches in the mountains and 1 to 9 inches in the lowlands occurred from Jan 6th through 8th.  Flooding was widespread over much of Western Washington.  Record flooding occurred on the Snoqualmie, Tolt, and North Fork Stillaguamish Rivers.  Near record flooding occurred on the Snohomish, Newaukum, and Skookumchuck Rivers.   Major flooding occurred on 18 rivers and 21 forecast points.  Surveys found an estimated 497 residences that were destroyed or suffered major damage, and another 2,340 residences that needed repairs.  Over 44,000 people were evacuated as a result of rising or high water as well as landslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused some flooding and landslides, which damaged several roads and about a dozen homes.  Along with the heavy rain, faulty drainage accounted for some flooding in Sekiu.  The waterfront trail in Port Angeles was closed due to fallen trees and small landslides. A bridge in western Clallam County was washed out, stranding a few families. There were numerous other county roads impassible from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain and snow melt led to flooding over portions of Chelan County.  The flooding prompted Chelan County commissioners to declare a State of Emergency. Part of highway 97 over Blewett Pass was washed away by the heavy rain.  A large mudslide also came down and covered 200 feet of the highway with six feet of mud, rock, and trees. This led to the closure of the pass for ten days.  In addition, a plugged culvert led to flooding on Butcher Creek Road near Highway two.  The begin and end time of this event is estimated.  The property damage from this event is estimated to be around 1.1 millon dollars. In Entiat, two homes were flooded by the Mad River due to an ice jam.

A strong ridge of high pressure over the area resulted in persistent freezing fog that lasted nearly two weeks.  This led to ice buildup and significant damage to power lines from Davenport to Almira on the Highway 2 corridor.  This was the most damaging weather event to hit Lincoln County since the November 1996 Ice Storm.  Avista had to replace at least 160 power poles, 1,400 insulators, and more than 60 transformers.  During the peak of the outages, around 6,800 customers were without power.

A strong winter storm produced strong winds in southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Pacific front produced high winds of 46 sustained winds along the southwest Washington Coast. Some other reports included winds gusts of 60 to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought widespread heavy snow accumulations to Eastern Washington.  This resulted in additional roof collapses in the Spokane area with about 50 collapses in Spokane county.  This resulted in an estimated damage amount of ten million dollars.  An estimated 200 Air and National Guard troops were called in to assist with snow removal.  Several road closures also resulted from the snow.  Numerous reports of five to nine inches of snow fell with higher amounts in the mountains.  Along the East Slopes of the North Washington Cascades, snow accumulations ranged from eight inches in Leavenworth to 15 inches at Holden Village.  In the Blue Mountains, a spotter five miles southwest of Anatone picked up 11 inches of accumulation.  This storm in combination with several winter storms in previous days resulted in major damage across parts of the area.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN STRAIT OF JUAN DE FUCA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system with a very cold air mass moved into western Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten inches of snow fell in parts of the North Olympic Peninsula. Port Angeles and Sequim school districts closed for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system accompanied by a very cold air mass dug from the offshore waters of Vancouver Island into southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow fell in Mason County, most of it above 200 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and unstable low pressure system brought numerous moderate to heavy snow showers to the palouse and Spokane area.  Several locations picked up between four to six inches of snow.  The heavy snow showers resulted in accumulations of an inch per hour which contributed to over 100 slide-offs and accidents in the Spokane area.

Numerous snow showers occurred in the Northeast Blue Mountains as a cold and unstable weather system tracked through the area.  Upslope flow also enhanced snow showers. A trained weather spotter four miles west of Anatone picked up 13 inches of snow.
An early spring storm brought heavy snow to the Northeast Blue Mountains, where persistent moist upslope flow brought a foot of snow to Ski Bluewood.

Strong downslope winds in the lee of the Cascades impacted the Wenatchee area and resulted in significant damage.  The strongest winds were observed in the Wenatchee Heights area on the south side of Wenatchee, where gusts as high as 78 MPH were recorded with even an unofficial gust of 98 mph.  These high winds blew off an entire roof.  Elsewhere, two windows were blown out of a building in downtown Wenatchee.  Numerous power outages resulted from the downslope windstorm.  Additionally, a carport was blown down along with over 200 trees.  Seven miles southwest of Quincy, an irrigation circle was blown across the Columbia River south of Crescent Bar.  Total property damage from this event was estimated at nearly $65,000.

A small, quick moving shortwave and associated frontal system brought a quick dose of heavy snow to the south Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches total snowfall was reported at the Sheep Canyon SNOTEL site on Mt. St. Helens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds mixed down to the surface behind the passage of a strong cold front in the Spokane area. Spokane International Airport recorded a peak wind gust of 66 miles per hour at 4:14 PM PST.  The winds damaged a transmission line, which cut power to 6,726 customers on Spokane's South Hill.

An early spring storm brought heavy snow to the northeast Blue Mountains and Palouse.  The Sourdough Gulch SNOTEL eight miles west of Anatone picked up 11 inches of accumulation.  On the Palouse, accumulations above 2500 feet ranged from three to six inches.

An early spring storm brought heavy snow to the northeast Blue Mountains and Palouse.  The Sourdough Gulch SNOTEL eight miles west of Anatone picked up 11 inches of accumulation.  On the Palouse, accumulations above 2500 feet ranged from three to six inches.
A potent late season frontal system brought heavy snow to the higher elevations of southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals for this event include: 29 inches at the June Lake SNOTEL; 24 inches at the Surprise Lakes SNOTEL; 29 inches at the Spencer Meadows SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight but compact low level circulation aided in generating a localized area of heavy snow over Northwest Spokane.  Total accumulations ranged anywhere from two to 12 inches, with the heaviest accumulations occurring on the Five Mile Prairie.  State Route 291 was closed for about two hours between Nine Mile Falls Dam and Suncrest after snow and ice led to several slide-offs on Big Sandy Hill.  Other areas of Spokane received around an inch or less of accumulation.
Ahead of a deep, incoming trough, a weather system brought snow to some higher elevations in southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen inches fell over the June Lake SNOTEL, located near Mt. St. Helens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter-like storm system moved into the Inland Northwest during the first few days of April.  A very unusual occurrence of low elevation snow was observed late on April first going into the early morning hours of the second, when a warm front brought widespread snowfall above 1500 to 2000 feet elevation.  Snow accumulations with the warm front ranged mostly from three to five inches.   Behind the warm front, snow showers fell in the Northeast Blue Mountains where snow accumulations persisted with Ski Bluewood picking up over a foot of total snow accumulation.  This was the last measurable snow event for the Spokane Airport, which brought the seasonal snowfall total to 97.7 inches, making it the snowiest winter on record.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES NORTHERN CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system with copious moisture and relatively high freezing levels brought snow to the higher elevations of the Olympics and North Cascades, mainly in the vicinity of Mt Baker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWAC station at Mt Baker recorded heavy snow (21 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system with copious moisture and relatively high freezing levels brought snow to the higher elevations of the Olympics and North Cascades, mainly in the vicinity of Mt Baker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWAC station at Hurricane Ridge recorded heavy snowfall (22 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of snow fell over the Snohomish County lowlands and in the east Puget Sound lowlands of King and Snohomish Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS trained spotters and the CoCoRAHS network indicate that 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the east Puget Sound lowlands in King and Snohomish Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of snow fell over the Snohomish County lowlands and in the east Puget Sound lowlands of King and Snohomish Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell over parts of Snohomish county, mainly from Everett to Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds over Snohomish and Island counties lasted for several hours. A woman was killed in the Monroe area when a tree fell on the minivan she was driving.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was killed, and a man injured, when a tree fell on their minivan while driving near Monroe.  A few other trees were blown down, one damaging a parked truck.  About 100 customers lost power.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow (up to 20 inches) occurred in the Northern and Central Cascades, mainly in the vicinity of Stevens Pass and Snoqualmie Pass. A Puget Sound Convergence Zone helped augment snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 20 inches of snow fell in the Snoqualmie Pass area.  The pass was closed for a few hours.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES NORTHERN CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow (up to 20 inches) occurred in the Northern and Central Cascades, mainly in the vicinity of Stevens Pass and Snoqualmie Pass. A Puget Sound Convergence Zone helped augment snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWAC station at Stevens Pass showed 16 inch snowfall in past 12 hours.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought another round of snowfall to the Cascades. A Puget Sound Convergence Zone enhanced snowfall especially at Stevens Pass, producing heavy snow for both the Northern and Central Cascades zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWAC station at Stevens Pass recorded 12 inches snowfall in 10 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm passed between Ephrata and Moses Lake.  Strong winds associated with the storm blew off a piece of sheet metal roofing.
An upper level low pressure area over southern Oregon brought abundant moisture and unstable conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches accompanied by small hail fell within 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm with rotation first spawned a funnel cloud and then a tornado, which briefly touched down just northeast of Davenport.  The funnel cloud was spotted 16 miles northwest of Odessa, followed by a weak tornado eight miles north-northeast of Davenport.  The EF1 tornado damaged two structures.  By the time the storm reached north Spokane County, the rotation had weakened and was not as evident. However, a localized area of strong downburst winds impacted an area located five miles west of Chattaroy where a portion of a metal roof was blown off an out-building near the intersection of Monroe and Crosscut roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail was reported from a severe thunderstorm ten miles northwest of Anatone in Cloverland, and four miles north of Colton.
Quarter-sized hail was reported from a severe thunderstorm ten miles northwest of Anatone in Cloverland, and four miles north of Colton.

Several large hail reports came from thunderstorms in Stevens County during the evening hours of July 12th.  Hail reports ranged from small marble to half dollar size in diameter, with the strongest storm occurring over Kettle Falls.  Penny size hail was also reported in Ford.

Windy weather affected the central Puget Sound region. Snow fell in the mountains and in the Hood Canal area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked out power to about 16,000 customers in the greater Seattle area.  Several trees were also knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Windy weather affected the central Puget Sound region. Snow fell in the mountains and in the Hood Canal area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 4-6 inches of snow fell over the Hood Canal area Sunday.  There were several car accidents, but no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Windy weather affected the central Puget Sound region. Snow fell in the mountains and in the Hood Canal area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind was measured at Tatoosh Island--62 KT sustained.  This translates to a 62 KT gust on the mainland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell in the Cascade foothills, mainly between Eatonville and Enumclaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell in the cascade foothills, mainly between Eatonville and Enumclaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Naches River at Naches rose slightly above flood stage with no damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Naches River at Naches crested at 17.13 feet on May 19 between 8:45 am PST and 11:30 am PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong marine push combined with thunderstorm outflow produced wind gusts to 54 mph near the Cascade foothills.  About 3300 customers lost power from fallen tree limbs on power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong marine push combined with thunderstorm outflow produced wind gusts to 54 mph near the Cascade foothills, from Yelm to Issaquah.  About 1200 customers lost power from fallen tree limbs on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong marine push combined with thunderstorm outflow produced wind gusts to 54 mph near the Cascade foothills.  About 3300 customers lost power from fallen tree limbs on power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong marine push combined with thunderstorm outflow produced wind gusts which knocked out power to nearly 5000 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm washed out Addington Road, 50 feet wide and three feet deep.

A thunderstorm containing very heavy rainfall led to flash flooding eight miles southeast of Northport.  A storm survey indicated mud and debris flow across Aladdin Road near Spirit.  The public confirmed that the road was impassible during the event.  Minor erosion of the road embankment was also observed approximately one mile east of Spirit along Aladdin Road.
Strong winds from a thunderstorm downed trees across Cedonia and Addy Roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff reported the public utility company had to clear the road and repair powerlines at Lesig Road and Locke Road and clear downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a car where a couple were taking shelter from a thunderstorm.  The lightning blew out two tires on the car and caused the engine to not start.
The Johnson Lake Fire, located six miles north of Nespelem, was a lightning caused fire that burned 955 acres.

The Union Valley Fire, located three miles north of Chelan, was a lightning caused fire that burned 768 acres.  The fire burned into the first day of August.  Between 120 and 140 residences received level two and three evacuation notices.

Very heavy rainfall from a slow-moving thunderstorm sent a 10 foot by 30 foot mud slide across a gravel road near Stemilt Creek just south of Wenatchee.  Mud damage was reported to driveways and an orchard.

A thunderstorm containing very heavy rainfall resulted in water running down Highway 20 between Colville and Tiger.  Water washed out a small berm and approximately one foot of the pavement along the shoulder.  It is estimated that this occurred near mile marker 368.

Breezy conditions resulted in rapid fire spread from a wildfire which burned an estimated 10,000 acres eight miles east of Highway 261 near Benge.  The fire burned old abandoned buildings, and an old homestead.  The end time and property damage from this event are estimated.

Abundant lightning triggered several fires in Grant County with the Grant County Board of Commissioners signing an emergency declaration to secure funding to suppress the fires.  The Grant County Complex, which consisted of several fires around Soap Lake, burned 2,000 acres of sagebrush and grass land.  About 15 fires were ignited from the lightning strikes throughout Grant County.

The combination of an unstable atmosphere and converging wind boundaries over Lincoln County spawned four tornadoes, as well as large hail and flash flooding.  This activity spread into northern Whitman County as well.  The tornadoes were observed in a 25 minute period between Wilbur and Creston near Highway 2.  All four tornadoes were non-supercell tornadoes and rated EF0.  All four tornadoes remained over wheat fields and thus resulted in no property damage.  Meanwhile, a severe thunderstorm produced quarter sized hail three miles west-southwest of Sprague.  Heavy rain also fell with the storms over Lincoln and northern Whitman County.  On Highway 23, about five miles northwest of Sprague, water covered portions of the highway between mile posts 48 and 53.  In northern Whitman County, mud and debris washed onto roads near Ewen, Colfax, and St. John.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado formed two miles south of Highway 2 between Wilbur and Creston.  This tornado tracked west before dissipating.  This was one of four tornadoes that occurred within a 25 minute period in the area.

In northern Whitman County, mud and debris washed onto roads near Ewen, Colfax, and St. John.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado was observed two miles south of Highway 2 between Creston and Wilbur.  This was one of four tornadoes that occurred within a 25 minute period.  This tornado tracked west-northwest and stayed just south of Ramsey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong downburst winds from a thunderstorm knocked out power to 2,000 customers in Clarkston when a tree blew down on a power line.  Additionally, a portable carport was blown into another power line.  Another tree was uprooted knocking down another power line and landing on a shop.

A slow-moving thunderstorm produced local heavy rainfall in Quincy and the north part of the Quincy Valley.  During a 15-minute period, strong winds, hail, and heavy rain significantly damaged crops.  A flash flood also damaged part of Road Q NW about two miles north of Quincy.

Very heavy rainfall from a slow-moving thunderstorm sent a 10 foot by 30 foot mud slide across a gravel road near Stemilt Creek just south of Wenatchee.  Mud damage was reported to driveways and an orchard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked from Priest Lake area in northern Idaho south-southwest into Newport.  In Newport, trees fell on several structures as this storm moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on structures in Newport after a severe thunderstorm tracked through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized high winds occurred during the early morning hours at Priest Rapids Dam where a peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded.  The time of these winds were estimated from surrounding observations.

Strong high pressure aloft led to a major heat event for western Washington.  The average temperature for July 2009 at Seattle-Tacoma airport was 69.5 degrees, the warmest July on record, and tying record for the warmest July on record in Seattle. During the heat wave, many other record and all-time record high temperatures were broken, including 103 degrees at Seattle-Tacoma airport, 104 at Olympia and 96 at Bellingham.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday July 29th, Olympia tied the all time record high of 104.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure aloft led to a major heat event for western Washington.  The average temperature for July 2009 at Seattle-Tacoma airport was 69.5 degrees, the warmest July on record, and tying record for the warmest July on record in Seattle. During the heat wave, many other record and all-time record high temperatures were broken, including 103 degrees at Seattle-Tacoma airport, 104 at Olympia and 96 at Bellingham.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday July 29th, Bellingham set a new all time record high of 96.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm caused a flash flood and debris flow that covered State Route 20. The debris flow was 300 yards wide and more than 10 feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm caused a flash flood and debris flow that covered State Route 20. The debris flow was 300 yards wide and more than 10 feet deep.  Two people were treated for minor injuries from the debris flow and their motorcycle had to be towed back to Marblemount.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds from a thunderstorm in the Cascade mountains knocked over several trees that blocked the Baker Lake Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Couple of dozen trees, some very large old growth, were blown down across several areas of the Baker Lake Highway. About 50 cars, were trapped behind until the road was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure aloft led to a major heat event for western Washington.  The average temperature for July 2009 at Seattle-Tacoma airport was 69.5 degrees, the warmest July on record, and tying record for the warmest July on record in Seattle. During the heat wave, many other record and all-time record high temperatures were broken, including 103 degrees at Seattle-Tacoma airport, 104 at Olympia and 96 at Bellingham.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the warmest July on record at Seattle-Tacoma airport.  On Wednesday (July 29) in Seattle, the high temperature reached 103 degrees (SeaTac Airport), a new all-time high.  A heat related death occurred when a 66 year old male died in Seattle. The downtown Bremerton library closed on Tuesday and Wednesday due to excessive heat.  Some UW librarys closed and several classes were held outside due to the lack of AC inside the classrooms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday July 29th, several locations were warmer than 100 degrees.  They include Granite Falls at 108, and Lake Stevens at 105.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds in Wenatchee gusting to between 55 and 60 miles per hour led to minor property damage.  Roof damage was reported to a building near the Wenatchee Airport.  The winds also blew siding off of a house.  The Wenatchee ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 56 mph at 11:42 PM Pacific Standard Time on October 3rd.
Strong winds from a thunderstorm downed trees across Cedonia and Addy Roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported downed trees across Cedonia and Addy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind near the cascade foothills knocked down trees across roads and into power lines.  A teenager was injured when a tree limb fell across her legs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 55 mph knocked down trees across roads and into power lines.  A teenager in North Bend was injured when a tree limb fell across her legs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast winds of 20 to 30 mph resulted in a severe dust storm with visibilities near zero over southeast Grant County and western Adams County, including the cities of  Othello, Warden, and Odessa.  A total of 11 indirect injuries in Grant County occurred due to motor vehicle collisions from the dust storm.  Widespread road closures resulted from the storm, including Interstate 90 between Moses Lake and Ritzville for most of the day on October 4th.

Northeast winds gusting to near 50 miles per hour contributed to power outages in the Deer Park and Clayton areas.  Trees were snapped off during the winds and landed on power lines which contributed to the couple of thousands of residences losing power.  The winds also snapped a power pole.

Freezing rain accumulated to around 1/4 inch 1 mile southwest of Prosser and 5 miles west of Richland.

A funnel cloud formed near Maple Valley for a few minutes.

Thunderstorms produced a funnel cloud in the Yakima area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred with the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Oden Road Fire, located eight miles northwest of Malott, was a lightning caused fire which burned 10,258 acres.  The fire destroyed two homes, an 1890's school house, and several barns and sheds.  About 300 residences were threatened by the fire.  The fire was most active during the evening hours of August 21st and the early early morning hours of August 22nd when winds pushed the fire downhill.  The fire burned into the only transmission line that brings power to the Methow Valley, knocking out power to 5,100 customers.  Highway 20 was closed for a period during the fire between Okanogan and Twisp.

A tornado touched down around 1619 PDT near the southeast corner of Lake Tapps and moved northeast around 36 mph for 16 minutes, lifting off the ground just northeast of Enumclaw.  There was also a lightning strike which damaged a home in Auburn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Confirmed EF1 tornado with estimated wind speed 100-110  mph. Maximum width was 170 yards. On ground intermittently over a 9.6 mile path length for 16 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire of unknown cause, was located six miles south of Nespelem, and burned 1,923 acres.  The exact time that the fire began and ended are unknown.

A deep low pressure area moved across the Cascade Mountains and across the Lower Columbia Basin to produce high winds. Wind gusts in mph included 14 miles west northwest of West Richland (64), Richland (64), and Juniper Dunes RAWS (61).

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE CENTRAL CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving landslide northwest of Naches covered a section of Highway 410 and damaged homes. The slide also diverted the Naches River which flooded several homes.

A tornado touched down around 1619 PDT near the southeast corner of Lake Tapps and moved northeast around 36 mph for 16 minutes, lifting off the ground just northeast of Enumclaw.  There was also a lightning strike which damaged a home in Auburn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree next to a home, causing damage to the home's siding and much of the electronics inside.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES NORTHERN CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to all three mountain zones. The heaviest amounts (up to 16 inches) occurred near Stevens Pass, helped by the Puget Sound Convergence Zone. The was the first major mountain snowfall of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NW Weather and Avalanche Center (NWAC) station at Stevens Pass reported 16 inches snow between 6 AM Oct 26 and 7 AM Oct 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific frontal system brought heavy snow to all three mountain zones. The heaviest amounts (up to 16 inches) occurred near Stevens Pass, helped by the Puget Sound Convergence Zone. The was the first major mountain snowfall of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NW Weather and Avalanche Center (NWAC) station at Hurricane Ridge reported 5 inches snow. SNOTEL stations in the Olympics supported higher snowfall amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, some containing damaging winds and nickel size hail, tracked from Lincoln County northeast into Stevens and Pend Oreille counties.  Several trees were uprooted or snapped off from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mature pines were blown over on highway between Rice and Gifford.
